turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg has the opinion of the Court in this morning in case 15-537, Bravo-Fernandez versus United States.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: This case concerns the issue-preclusive effect of a jury's acquittal verdict that conflicts with the same jury's guilty verdict on a related count in the same case. The issue-preclusion principle bars re-litigation of an issue of fact or law necessarily resolved by a prior judgment. In 1970, in Ashe v. Swenson, this Court read the Double Jeopardy Clause to include an issue-preclusion component. A defendant seeking to foreclose a second prosecution on this ground bears the burden of establishing that the issue he seeks to shield from re-trial was actually decided by a prior jury's verdict of acquittal. When the same jury confronting two or more discreet counts returns inconsistent verdicts of conviction and acquittal on the key issue, a defendant cannot meet that burden. In such a case, this Court held in 1984 in United States against Powell, both verdicts stand. The Government is barred by the Double Jeopardy Clause from challenging the acquittal, but because the verdicts are rationally irreconcilable, the acquittal gains no preclusive force over the count on which the defendant was convicted. Issue preclusion, however, does attend a jury's verdict of acquittal if the same jury in the same case hangs. That is, fails to reach a verdict on a different count turning on the same issue of ultimate fact. In that situation, we held in 2009 in Yeager v. United States, the acquittal does have preclusive force. A hung count reveals nothing more than a jury's failure to reach a decision. The non-decision, in other words, is a blank. It supplies no evidence of jury inconsistency. Therefore, it has no place in the issue-preclusion analysis. In this case, a jury convicted petitioners Juan Bravo-Fernandez and Hector Martinez-Maldonado of bribery in violation of 18 U.S.C. § 666. Simultaneously, the jury acquitted them of conspiring to violate that statute and traveling in interstate commerce in furtherance of the charged bribery. Because the only contested issue in each count was whether Bravo and Martinez had engaged in bribery in violation of §666, the jury's verdicts were logically incompatible. Without Powell, the inconsistent verdicts decision would control. And in view of the inconsistency, the acquittals would not preclude retrial on the counts of conviction. But there is a wrinkle. Unlike the guilty verdicts in Powell, petitioner's convictions in this case were later vacated on appeal because of error in the court's instructions to the jury, error entirely unrelated to the verdict's inconsistency. On remand, relying on the appeals court's vacatur of the convictions, Bravo and Martinez moved for judgments of acquittal on the stand-alone §666 bribery charges. Just as hung-to-hung counts have no place in issue-preclusion Analysis, they urged, so vacated guilty verdicts should not figure in that analysis. The district court denied the motions and the First Circuit affirmed. The eventual invalidation of petitioner's §666 bribery convictions, the Court of Appeals held, did not undermine Powell's instruction that issue-preclusion drops out when the same jury returns logically inconsistent verdicts. We affirm the First Circuit's judgment. When a jury hangs, one cannot know why it failed to return a verdict. A host of reasons, we recognized in Yeager, could account for a hung jury; sharp disagreement, confusion about the issues, sheer exhaustion after a long trial, for example; but admitted inconsistency in a jury's verdicts is a reality not erased by vacatur of a conviction for entirely unrelated trial error. The conviction still reveals that the jury acted irrationally; whether because of mistake, compromise or lenity, petitioner's acquittal therefore do not support application of issue-preclusion. So, we bracket this case with Powell, which governs inconsistent verdicts, not Yeager dealing with hung counts. The Double Jeopardy Clause, we hold, does not bar re-trial of defendants like Bravo and Martinez when a jury has returned inconsistent verdicts of conviction and acquittal. The inconsistency remains even if the convictions are later vacated for legal error unrelated to the inconsistency. The Court's opinion is unanimous. Justice Thomas has also filed a concurring opinion.
